    "content": "{"content":"commit 64dd89ae01f2708a508e028c28b7906e4702a9a7\nAuthor: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>\nDate:   Mon Dec 15 12:57:53 2025 -0500\n\n    mm/block/fs: remove laptop_mode\n    \n    Laptop mode was introduced to save battery, by delaying and consolidating\n    writes and thereby maximize the time rotating hard drives wouldn't have to\n    spin.\n    \n    Luckily, rotating hard drives, with their high spin-up times and power\n    draw, are a thing of the past for battery-powered devices.  Reclaim has\n    also since changed to not write single filesystem pages anymore, and\n    regular filesystem writeback is lumpy by design.\n    \n    The juice doesn't appear worth the squeeze anymore.  The footprint of the\n    feature is small, but nevertheless it's a complicating factor in mm,\n    block, filesystems.  Developers don't think about it, and it likely hasn't\n    been tested with new reclaim and writeback changes in years.\n    \n    Let's sunset it.  Keep the sysctl with a deprecation warning around for a\n    few more cycles, but remove all functionality behind it.\n    \n    [akpm@linux-foundation.org: fix Documentation/admin-guide/laptops/index.rst]\n    Link: https://lkml.kernel.org/r/20251216185201.GH905277@cmpxchg.org\n    Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>\n    Suggested-by: Christoph Hellwig <hch@infradead.org>\n    Reviewed-by: Christoph Hellwig <hch@lst.de>\n    Acked-by: Jens Axboe <axboe@kernel.dk>\n    Reviewed-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>\n    Acked-by: Michal Hocko <mhocko@suse.com>\n    Cc: Deepanshu Kartikey <kartikey406@gmail.com>\n    Signed-off-by: Andrew Morton <akpm@linux-foundation.org>\n\ndiff --git a/Documentation/admin-guide/laptops/index.rst b/Documentation/admin-guide/laptops/index.rst\nindex 6432c251dc95..c0b911d05c59 100644\n--- a/Documentation/admin-guide/laptops/index.rst\n+++ b/Documentation/admin-guide/laptops/index.rst\n@@ -10,7 +10,6 @@ Laptop Drivers\n    alienware-wmi\n    asus-laptop\n    disk-shock-protection\n-   laptop-mode\n    lg-laptop\n    samsung-galaxybook\n    sony-laptop\ndiff --git a/Documentation/admin-guide/laptops/laptop-mode.rst b/Documentation/admin-guide/laptops/laptop-mode.rst\ndeleted file mode 100644\nindex 66eb9cd918b5..000000000000\n--- a/Documentation/admin-guide/laptops/laptop-mode.rst\n+++ /dev/null\n@@ -1,770 +0,0 @@\n-===============================================\n-How to conserve battery power using laptop-mode\n-===============================================\n-\n-Document Author: Bart Samwel (bart@samwel.tk)\n-\n-Date created: January 2, 2004\n-\n-Last modified: December 06, 2004\n-\n-Introduction\n-------------\n-\n-Laptop mode is used to minimize the time that the hard disk needs to be spun up,\n-to conserve battery power on laptops. It has been reported to cause significant\n-power savings.\n-\n-.. Contents\n-\n-   * Introduction\n-   * Installation\n-   * Caveats\n-   * The Details\n-   * Tips & Tricks\n-   * Control script\n-   * ACPI integration\n-   * Monitoring tool\n-\n-\n-Installation\n-------------\n-\n-To use laptop mode, you don't need to set any kernel configuration options\n-or anything. Simply install all the files included in this document, and\n-laptop mode will automatically be started when you're on battery. For\n-your convenience, a tarball containing an installer can be downloaded at:\n-\n-\thttp://www.samwel.tk/laptop_mode/laptop_mode/\n-\n-To configure laptop mode, you need to edit the configuration file, which is\n-located in /etc/default/laptop-mode on Debian-based systems, or in\n-/etc/sysconfig/laptop-mode on other systems.\n-\n-Unfortunately, automatic enabling of laptop mode does not work for\n-laptops that don't have ACPI. On those laptops, you need to start laptop\n-mode manually. To start laptop mode, run \"laptop_mode start\", and to\n-stop it, run \"laptop_mode stop\". (Note: The laptop mode tools package now\n-has experimental support for APM, you might want to try that first.)\n-\n-\n-Caveats\n--------\n-\n-* The downside of laptop mode is that you have a chance of losing up to 10\n-  minutes of work. If you cannot afford this, don't use it! The supplied ACPI\n-  scripts automatically turn off laptop mode when the battery almost runs out,\n-  so that you won't lose any data at the end of your battery life.\n-\n-* Most desktop hard drives have a very limited lifetime measured in spindown\n-  cycles, typically about 50.000 times (it's usually listed on the spec sheet).\n-  Check your drive's rating, and don't wear down your drive's lifetime if you\n-  don't need to.\n-\n-* If you mount some of your ext3 filesystems with the -n option, then\n-  the control script will not be able to remount them correctly. You must set\n-  DO_REMOUNTS=0 in the control script, otherwise it will remount them with the\n-  wrong options -- or it will fail because it cannot write to /etc/mtab.\n-\n-* If you have your filesystems listed as type \"auto\" in fstab, like I did, then\n-  the control script will not recognize them as filesystems that need remounting.\n-  You must list the filesystems with their true type instead.\n-\n-* It has been reported that some versions of the mutt mail client use file access\n-  times to determine whether a folder contains new mail. If you use mutt and\n-  experience this, you must disable the noatime remounting by setting the option\n-  DO_REMOUNT_NOATIME to 0 in the configuration file.\n-\n-\n-The Details\n------------\n-\n-Laptop mode is controlled by the knob /proc/sys/vm/laptop_mode. This knob is\n-present for all kernels that have the laptop mode patch, regardless of any\n-configuration options. When the knob is set, any physical disk I/O (that might\n-have caused the hard disk to spin up) causes Linux to flush all dirty blocks. The\n-result of this is that after a disk has spun down, it will not be spun up\n-anymore to write dirty blocks, because those blocks had already been written\n-immediately after the most recent read operation. The value of the laptop_mode\n-knob determines the time between the occurrence of disk I/O and when the flush\n-is triggered. A sensible value for the knob is 5 seconds. Setting the knob to\n-0 disables laptop mode.\n-\n-To increase the effectiveness of the laptop_mode strategy, the laptop_mode\n-control script increases dirty_expire_centisecs and dirty_writeback_centisecs in\n-/proc/sys/vm to about 10 minutes (by default), which means that pages that are\n-dirtied are not forced to be written to disk as often. The control script also\n-changes the dirty background ratio, so that background writeback of dirty pages\n-is not done anymore. Combined with a higher commit value (also 10 minutes) for\n-ext3 filesystem (also done automatically by the control script),\n-this results in concentration of disk activity in a small time interval which\n-occurs only once every 10 minutes, or whenever the disk is forced to spin up by\n-a cache miss. The disk can then be spun down in the periods of inactivity.\n-\n-\n-Configuration\n--------------\n-\n-The laptop mode configuration file is located in /etc/default/laptop-mode on\n-Debian-based systems, or in /etc/sysconfig/laptop-mode on other systems. It\n-contains the following options:\n-\n-MAX_AGE:\n-\n-Maximum time, in seconds, of hard drive spindown time that you are\n-comfortable with. Worst case, it's possible that you could lose this\n-amount of work if your battery fails while you're in laptop mode.\n-\n-MINIMUM_BATTERY_MINUTES:\n-\n-Automatically disable laptop mode if the remaining number of minutes of\n-battery power is less than this value. Default is 10 minutes.\n-\n-AC_HD/BATT_HD:\n-\n-The idle timeout that should be set on your hard drive when laptop mode\n-is active (BATT_HD) and when it is not active (AC_HD). The defaults are\n-20 seconds (value 4) for BATT_HD  and 2 hours (value 244) for AC_HD. The\n-possible values are those listed in the manual page for \"hdparm\" for the\n-\"-S\" option.\n-\n-HD:\n-\n-The devices for which the spindown timeout should be adjusted by laptop mode.\n-Default is /dev/hda. If you specify multiple devices, separate them by a space.\n-\n-READAHEAD:\n-\n-Disk readahead, in 512-byte sectors, while laptop mode is active. A large\n-readahead can prevent disk accesses for things like executable pages (which are\n-loaded on demand while the application executes) and sequentially accessed data\n-(MP3s).\n-\n-DO_REMOUNTS:\n-\n-The control script automatically remounts any mounted journaled filesystems\n-with appropriate commit interval options. When this option is set to 0, this\n-feature is disabled.\n-\n-DO_REMOUNT_NOATIME:\n-\n-When remounting, should the filesystems be remounted with the noatime option?\n-Normally, this is set to \"1\" (enabled), but there may be programs that require\n-access time recording.\n-\n-DIRTY_RATIO:\n-\n-The percentage of memory that is allowed to contain \"dirty\" or unsaved data\n-before a writeback is forced, while laptop mode is active. Corresponds to\n-the /proc/sys/vm/dirty_ratio sysctl.\n-\n-DIRTY_BACKGROUND_RATIO:\n-\n-The percentage of memory that is allowed to contain \"dirty\" or unsaved data\n-after a forced writeback is done due to an exceeding of DIRTY_RATIO. Set\n-this nice and low. This corresponds to the /proc/sys/vm/dirty_background_ratio\n-sysctl.\n-\n-Note that the behaviour of dirty_background_ratio is quite different\n-when laptop mode is active and when it isn't. When laptop mode is inactive,\n-dirty_background_ratio is the threshold percentage at which background writeouts\n-start taking place. When laptop mode is active, however, background writeouts\n-are disabled, and the dirty_background_ratio only determines how much writeback\n-is done when dirty_ratio is reached.\n-\n-DO_CPU:\n-\n-Enable CPU frequency scaling when in laptop mode. (Requires CPUFreq to be setup.\n-See Documentation/admin-guide/pm/cpufreq.rst for more info. Disabled by default.)\n-\n-CPU_MAXFREQ:\n-\n-When on battery, what is the maximum CPU speed that the system should use? Legal\n-values are \"slowest\" for the slowest speed that your CPU is able to operate at,\n-or a value listed in /sys/devices/system/cpu/cpu0/cpufreq/scaling_available_frequencies.\n-\n-\n-Tips & Tricks\n--------------\n-\n-* Bartek Kania reports getting up to 50 minutes of extra battery life (on top\n-  of his regular 3 to 3.5 hours) using a spindown time of 5 seconds (BATT_HD=1).\n-\n-* You can spin down the disk while playing MP3, by setting disk readahead\n-  to 8MB (READAHEAD=16384). Effectively, the disk will read a complete MP3 at\n-  once, and will then spin down while the MP3 is playing. (Thanks to Bartek\n-  Kania.)\n-\n-* Drew Scott Daniels observed: \"I don't know why, but when I decrease the number\n-  of colours that my display uses it consumes less battery power. I've seen\n-  this on powerbooks too. I hope that this is a piece of information that\n-  might be useful to the Laptop Mode patch or its users.\"\n-\n-* In syslog.conf, you can prefix entries with a dash `-` to omit syncing the\n-  file after every logging. When you're using laptop-mode and your disk doesn't\n-  spin down, this is a likely culprit.\n-\n-* Richard Atterer observed that laptop mode does not work well with noflushd\n-  (http://noflushd.sourceforge.net/), it seems that noflushd prevents laptop-mode\n-  from doing its thing.\n-\n-* If you're worried about your data, you might want to consider using a USB\n-  memory stick or something like that as a \"working area\". (Be aware though\n-  that flash memory can only handle a limited number of writes, and overuse\n-  may wear out your memory stick pretty quickly. Do _not_ use journalling\n-  filesystems on flash memory sticks.)\n-\n-\n-Configuration file for control and ACPI battery scripts\n--------------------------------------------------------\n-\n-This allows the tunables to be changed for the scripts via an external\n-configuration file\n-\n-It should be installed as /etc/default/laptop-mode on Debian, and as\n-/etc/sysconfig/laptop-mode on Red Hat, SUSE, Mandrake, and other work-alikes.\n-\n-Config file::\n-\n-  # Maximum time, in seconds, of hard drive spindown time that you are\n-  # comfortable with. Worst case, it's possible that you could lose this\n-  # amount of work if your battery fails you while in laptop mode.\n-  #MAX_AGE=600\n-\n-  # Automatically disable laptop mode when the number of minutes of battery\n-  # that you have left goes below this threshold.\n-  MINIMUM_BATTERY_MINUTES=10\n-\n-  # Read-ahead, in 512-byte sectors. You can spin down the disk while playing MP3/OGG\n-  # by setting the disk readahead to 8MB (READAHEAD=16384). Effectively, the disk\n-  # will read a complete MP3 at once, and will then spin down while the MP3/OGG is\n-  # playing.\n-  #READAHEAD=4096\n-\n-  # Shall we remount journaled fs. with appropriate commit interval? (1=yes)\n-  #DO_REMOUNTS=1\n-\n-  # And shall we add the \"noatime\" option to that as well? (1=yes)\n-  #DO_REMOUNT_NOATIME=1\n-\n-  # Dirty synchronous ratio.  At this percentage of dirty pages the process\n-  # which\n-  # calls write() does its own writeback\n-  #DIRTY_RATIO=40\n-\n-  #\n-  # Allowed dirty background ratio, in percent.  Once DIRTY_RATIO has been\n-  # exceeded, the kernel will wake flusher threads which will then reduce the\n-  # amount of dirty memory to dirty_background_ratio.  Set this nice and low,\n-  # so once some writeout has commenced, we do a lot of it.\n-  #\n-  #DIRTY_BACKGROUND_RATIO=5\n-\n-  # kernel default dirty buffer age\n-  #DEF_AGE=30\n-  #DEF_UPDATE=5\n-  #DEF_DIRTY_BACKGROUND_RATIO=10\n-  #DEF_DIRTY_RATIO=40\n-  #DEF_XFS_AGE_BUFFER=15\n-  #DEF_XFS_SYNC_INTERVAL=30\n-  #DEF_XFS_BUFD_INTERVAL=1\n-\n-  # This must be adjusted manually to the value of HZ in the running kernel\n-  # on 2.4, until the XFS people change their 2.4 external interfaces to work in\n-  # centisecs. This can be automated, but it's a work in progress that still\n-  # needs# some fixes. On 2.6 kernels, XFS uses USER_HZ instead of HZ for\n-  # external interfaces, and that is currently always set to 100. So you don't\n-  # need to change this on 2.6.\n-  #XFS_HZ=100\n-\n-  # Should the maximum CPU frequency be adjusted down while on battery?\n-  # Requires CPUFreq to be setup.\n-  # See Documentation/admin-guide/pm/cpufreq.rst for more info\n-  #DO_CPU=0\n-\n-  # When on battery what is the maximum CPU speed that the system should\n-  # use? Legal values are \"slowest\" for the slowest speed that your\n-  # CPU is able to operate at, or a value listed in:\n-  # /sys/devices/system/cpu/cpu0/cpufreq/scaling_available_frequencies\n-  # Only applicable if DO_CPU=1.\n-  #CPU_MAXFREQ=slowest\n-\n-  # Idle timeout for your hard drive (man hdparm for valid values, -S option)\n-  # Default is 2 hours on AC (AC_HD=244) and 20 seconds for battery (BATT_HD=4).\n-  #AC_HD=244\n-  #BATT_HD=4\n-\n-  # The drives for which to adjust the idle timeout.     "thought": "**Analysis of the Reintroduced Writeback Logic**

Okay, so I'm diving into this patch that reintroduces a check for starting background writeback in `balance_dirty_pages`. The initial commit effectively removed this check, and I see now that it was a problem. It was originally:

```c
		/* Start writeback even when in laptop mode */
		if (unlikely(!writeback_in_progress(wb)))
			wb_start_background_writeback(wb);
```

The removal of `laptop_mode` caused this check to be completely removed, leaving only the primary dirty threshold check:

```c
		if (nr_dirty > gdtc->bg_thresh && !writeback_in_progress(wb))
			wb_start_background_writeback(wb);
```

The concern is that the primary check doesn't handle cases where throttling is necessary even when the global dirty page count (`nr_dirty`) is below the threshold, such as with `strictlimit` BDIs and memcg-based throttling. In these scenarios, the writeback process might never start without the reintroduced check. So the patch adding it back makes sense:

```c
		if (unlikely(!writeback_in_progress(wb)))
			wb_start_background_writeback(wb);
```

Now, I need to verify there aren't any potential problems with this addition. The code is placed within a `for(;;)` loop inside `balance_dirty_pages`. Let's step through the logic.  The crucial part is the `writeback_in_progress(wb)` check and the call to `wb_start_background_writeback(wb)`.  The `writeback_in_progress()` function is an atomic read of the `WB_writeback_running` bit in `wb->state`. `wb_start_background_writeback(wb)` wakes up the flusher thread, which is done through `wb_wakeup(wb)`, which then does `mod_delayed_work`.  This calls `spin_lock_irq` but immediately releases.

The question then is whether there's some kind of interrupt/preemption issue with this lock/sleep call. It's clear that `balance_dirty_pages` can sleep due to `io_schedule_timeout()` which is key. The `wb_wakeup` function calls `mod_delayed_work`, which means that it may be a re-queue.

My main concern now is the spinlock: `spin_lock_irq(&wb->work_lock)` which disables interrupts.  Is there a scenario where `wb_wakeup` could be called with interrupts disabled? But `balance_dirty_pages` is sleepable, which means it runs in process context with interrupts enabled. So `spin_unlock_irq` will re-enable them.

I also need to consider if `balance_dirty_pages` is ever called while holding a spinlock or an RCU lock.  It explicitly uses `io_schedule_timeout()`, so it *must* be called from a context that can sleep, which means it cannot hold spinlocks. The documentation specifically mentions "balance_dirty_pages() must be called without holding any locks".  There is a potential for lock contention on `wb->work_lock` with multiple threads calling `balance_dirty_pages`, and potentially competing on this lock.  However, this issue existed before the patch. It's functionally identical to the logic before the `laptop_mode` commit.

Finally, I need to double-check for lock ordering issues or RCU issues.  Since no locks are held when `wb_wakeup` is called, no deadlock is possible. It seems the re-introduction of the writeback start condition is functionally correct and doesn't introduce any new significant locking issues.

My initial thought was, "Is there a problem with calling `wb_start_background_writeback(wb)` without checking if `wb` is still valid?" But then I remembered that `wb` *is* the writeback structure passed into `balance_dirty_pages`, so it's guaranteed to be alive during the function's execution. `wb_start_background_writeback` wakes up the flusher thread, which is fine as long as `wb` is alive. I double-checked that `writeback_in_progress(wb)` uses `test_bit`, so it's atomic, which eliminates any race conditions there.

Then, I started considering a potential infinite loop scenario within `balance_dirty_pages`. If `unlikely(!writeback_in_progress(wb))` is true, it calls `wb_start_background_writeback(wb)`. After that, it flushes foreign memcgs, calculates limits, then has the `io_schedule_timeout(pause)` call. No, no infinite tight loop here. It's sleeping, so it's not going to monopolize the CPU.

Now, the commit message: "Reinstate the unconditional writeback start so that writeback is guaranteed to be running whenever IO needs to be throttled,"... and it's fixing something related to `laptop_mode` removal.

If we *aren't* free running (meaning we're near the dirty limit even if we're still below the global background threshold, due to memcg or strict limit), we hit the added code and *do* start writeback. That's exactly what the commit intends: start writeback when throttling is needed.

I thought, "Are there any negative effects of calling `wb_start_background_writeback` unconditionally on every loop iteration where `!writeback_in_progress`?" But, no, it's fine. `writeback_in_progress` will eventually become true once the flusher thread starts writing.

If the flusher thread starts and the threshold is not met, what happens? Let's check the code: `wb_workfn` calls `wb_do_writeback`. If the flusher thread sees that `nr_dirty < bg_thresh`, does it exit? Does it do *anything*?

    "thought": "**The Writeback Throttle Bug**

Okay, let me break this down. `wb_check_background_flush` is the key. It hinges on `wb_over_bg_thresh(wb)`. So, what if that function *returns* false? Well, the flusher thread bails out, `WB_writeback_running` gets cleared, and the thread exits. That leaves `writeback_in_progress` as false, right? Then `balance_dirty_pages`, after its timeout, wakes up, loops, sees `!writeback_in_progress` is true (because the flusher just quit), and *re-invokes* `wb_start_background_writeback(wb)`. That should start the flusher again.

Yes, that's it! The flusher thread wakes, sets `WB_writeback_running` and starts all over, running `wb_over_bg_thresh(wb)` again. But wait... why was `balance_dirty_pages` throttling if `wb_over_bg_thresh(wb)` was initially false? Ah, here we go... This is the bug!

The commit text illuminates it: We can throttle because of strictlimits or memcg. In (a), throttling is calculated using per-wb thresholds. The per-wb threshold can be exceeded even when the global threshold isn't. In (b), memcg uses its own dirty count and thresholds that can trigger throttling *independent* of the global thresholds. So, even though `wb_over_bg_thresh(wb)` might evaluate to false *globally*, `balance_dirty_pages` might throttle because of memcg or strictlimits and calls `wb_start_background_writeback(wb)`.

But all that function does is queue the delayed work, which, when it runs, will hit `wb_do_writeback` then `wb_check_background_flush` and then, of course, `wb_over_bg_thresh(wb)`. So, what's `wb_over_bg_thresh(wb)` even doing when the other throttling factors are in play? I have a feeling that is key to isolating the flaw in the logic here.

    "thought": "**Critical Analysis of the Patch**

Okay, here's my assessment. Initially, I was satisfied. The code seemed straightforward: adding a check to unconditionally start background writeback in `balance_dirty_pages` if it wasn't already running. The context was clear – addressing the scenarios missed by the global dirty threshold check, specifically, `strictlimit` BDIs and memcg-based throttling. The patch's goal was to ensure writeback starts when required by these specific scenarios, even if the global dirty count was low. I went through the standard checks: no obvious sleeping in atomic context, missing unlocks, etc. I also meticulously followed the review directive, refusing to take existing code at face value.

However, a deeper dive revealed a critical flaw in the existing logic, which the patch _doesn't_ fix! By taking a deeper look, I found: when the global dirty count is *below* the background threshold, *but* the memcg dirty count *exceeds* its background threshold (or similarly in `strictlimit` scenarios), the existing `balance_dirty_pages` *breaks* out of its loop if both global and memcg are freerun, and *doesn't* call `wb_start_background_writeback`. This behavior means background writeback is DELAYED. The code is only reached if the `freerun` threshold is crossed. This essentially makes the background thresholds ineffective for memcg and strictlimit. The flusher *should* start immediately once the dirty pages exceed `bg_thresh`. The freerun logic is for the *throttling* of page producers, not the initiation of background writeback! The current code is broken in this regard. This means that a memcg or strictlimit BDI can be *over* its background threshold, but the writeback thread won't be started until the dirty count crosses the freerun threshold. This delay could result in more dirty pages than intended, and potentially stalls on the application. The patch, while seemingly correct, doesn't address or resolve this fundamental problem in the original code.

Thus, the patch itself is not the source of the problem. It is correct in its narrow scope, but the underlying issue, where background writeback is delayed in the memcg/strictlimit case, remains.

    "role": "assistant",
    "thought": "**Analysis of the Writeback Throttle Fix**

Okay, so I'm diving into this writeback throttling issue. It seems like the core problem revolves around delayed writeback scheduling interacting poorly with high dirty page generation rates, particularly when using strict limits or memcg-based throttling.

Initially, `__mark_inode_dirty` might trigger a delayed writeback via `wb_wakeup_delayed`. This delays writeback for a short period. The issue is that if pages are dirtied *very* quickly (e.g., 1GB/s), we can hit the "freerun" or "throttle" thresholds *before* the delayed writeback kicks in. Crucially, the code checks the *global* dirty page threshold first. If we are using strictlimits or memcg, we are NOT exceeding the global threshold, which means we *don't* start the background writeback immediately. This leads to waiting, potentially for a full 5-second delay, even though pages are piling up.

The original code then had *another* check to start writeback if none was in progress, but that was removed in a previous commit. So we are waiting on a timer that hasn't kicked in yet.

This patch fixes this by restoring the unconditional start if writeback is not already in progress. But is it safe?

The important check is:

```c
		if (unlikely(!writeback_in_progress(wb)))
			wb_start_background_writeback(wb);
```

This is placed *after* the `free_running` check. That's good! It means this conditional check only runs *if* we are approaching the dirty page limits. If we are not free running, we want writeback to happen *ASAP*.

`writeback_in_progress(wb)` seems safe; it uses a lockless check. `wb_start_background_writeback(wb)` is safe; wakes up the queue immediately (delay 0).

I see no immediate issues with incorrect variable usage, atomic context conflicts, lock order violations or resource management. I am also not concerned about RCU violations; there's no RCU usage here.

The key question is the memory barrier implications of `writeback_in_progress`. While `test_bit` used by this function is atomic, it doesn't guarantee a full memory barrier. But given the writeback thread is concurrent and uses `set_bit`, and that we're only checking to avoid unnecessary calls, this should be fine. `mod_delayed_work` handles being called concurrently or unnecessarily. The existing `mem_cgroup_flush_foreign(wb)` call comes after the introduced check, so no problems there.

I've also checked that the `bdi_writeback *wb` argument is valid. It's passed correctly through the call chain and holds a valid reference. No use-after-free issues.

Now, one crucial point: the loop.

```c
	for (;;) {
		...
		if (gdtc->freerun && (!mdtc || mdtc->freerun)) {
			...
			break;
		}

		if (unlikely(!writeback_in_progress(wb)))
			wb_start_background_writeback(wb);

		mem_cgroup_flush_foreign(wb);
		...
		pause = max_pause;
		pause = min(pause, min_pause);
		if (unlikely(fatal_signal_pending(current)))
			break;

		__set_current_state(TASK_KILLABLE);
		io_schedule_timeout(pause);
	}
```

If `writeback_in_progress(wb)` consistently returns false, we're calling `wb_start_background_writeback(wb)` *every* loop iteration. Is this bad?

The `io_schedule_timeout(pause)` call at the bottom of the loop introduces a delay. `pause` is bounded by `min_pause`, which is at least 1 jiffy. The loop *sleeps* at least one jiffy per iteration. Calling `wb_start_background_writeback(wb)` every jiffy while `writeback_in_progress` is false is fine. It just involves a fast lock acquisition and `mod_delayed_work`.

Finally, what if `BDP_ASYNC` is passed to `balance_dirty_pages_ratelimited_flags`? The code handles `BDP_ASYNC` appropriately.

Overall, the patch looks correct. It restores a crucial check to avoid throttling in the face of rapid dirty page generation. The added call in the loop looks harmless.
